title: DHCP - Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol
description: Master DHCP server configuration, scope management, lease management, and troubleshooting. Deploy DHCP across networks with proper subnetting, reservations, and relay agents.
starterCode:
# DHCP EXERCISE
# Configure dynamic IP address allocation

## SCENARIO: TechCorp Network Expansion
## - Organization: 500 employees, 3 office locations
## - Challenge: Automatic IP assignment, centralized management
## - Current: Manual IP assignment (administrative nightmare)
## - Goal: Implement DHCP for all locations

## PART 1: DHCP FUNDAMENTALS
## Q1: What is DHCP?
##     Definition: _______ (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol)
##     Purpose: _______ (automatically assign IP addresses)
##     Benefits: _______ (no manual configuration, centralized)
##     
##     Components:
##     - Server: Maintains pool of addresses
##     - Client: Requests IP on startup
##     - Process: Allocation, lease, renewal

## Q2: DHCP vs Static IP:
##     Static IP (manual):
##     - Assignment: _______ (administrator configures)
##     - Flexibility: Low (change = reconfigure)
##     - Reliability: High (won't change)
##     - Use: Servers, printers (need consistent address)
##     - Admin burden: _______ (high, every device)
##     
##     Dynamic IP (DHCP):
##     - Assignment: _______ (server automatically assigns)
##     - Flexibility: High (roaming, temporary devices)
##     - Reliability: Depends on DHCP server
##     - Use: Desktops, laptops, temporary devices
##     - Admin burden: _______ (low, centralized)

## Q3: DHCP process (DORA):
##     Step 1: Discover _______ (client broadcasts request)
##     - Packet: "Any DHCP servers out there?"
##     - Scope: Broadcast to entire subnet
##     - Message type: DHCPDISCOVER
##     
##     Step 2: Offer _______ (server offers address)
##     - Packet: "You can use 192.168.1.100 for 8 hours"
##     - Sent: Unicast to client
##     - Message type: DHCPOFFER
##     
##     Step 3: Request _______ (client accepts offer)
##     - Packet: "I accept 192.168.1.100"
##     - Scope: Broadcast (other servers cancel offers)
##     - Message type: DHCPREQUEST
##     
##     Step 4: Acknowledge _______ (server confirms)
##     - Packet: "Confirmed, lease until 10 AM"
##     - Sent: Unicast to client
##     - Message type: DHCPACK

## Q4: DHCP lease process:
##     Lease duration: _______ (typically 8 hours to 7 days)
##     
##     Timeline:
##     - T=0: IP assigned, lease begins
##     - T=50% (4 hours): Renewal attempt (T1)
##     - T=87.5% (7 hours): Rebinding attempt (T2)
##     - T=100% (8 hours): Lease expires
##     
##     Renewal process:
##     - Attempt: Contact original DHCP server
##     - If available: Renew lease (extends 8 more hours)
##     - If unavailable: Try other servers (rebinding)
##     - If no response: Keep using IP (grace period)
##     - Expiration: Release IP after lease ends

## Q5: DHCP scope configuration:
##     Network: 192.168.1.0/24 (256 total addresses)
##     
##     Scope configuration:
##     - Start address: _______ (192.168.1.100)
##     - End address: _______ (192.168.1.200)
##     - Range: 101 addresses available
##     - Reserved: Gateway (192.168.1.1), etc.
##     - Lease duration: _______ (8 hours)
##     
##     Exclusion list:
##     - 192.168.1.1: Gateway
##     - 192.168.1.5: DNS server
##     - 192.168.1.10: Print server
##     - Reason: These need fixed IPs

## PART 2: DHCP SERVER CONFIGURATION
## Q6: DHCP server roles:
##     Primary server:
##     - Role: _______ (main server, owns scopes)
##     - Availability: Must be up
##     - Failure: Network cannot get IPs
##     - Backup: Secondary server needed
##     
##     Secondary server:
##     - Role: _______ (backup, shares scopes)
##     - Availability: Activated if primary fails
##     - Configuration: Mirrors primary scopes
##     - Split: Typically 70/30 or 80/20
##     
##     Failover:
##     - 80/20 split: Primary has 80%, secondary 20%
##     - Reason: _______ (faster primary response)
##     - Redundancy: If primary fails, 20% still available

## Q7: DHCP scope for multiple subnets:
##     TechCorp offices:
##     - HQ (192.168.1.0/24): 200 employees
##     - Branch A (192.168.2.0/24): 100 employees
##     - Branch B (192.168.3.0/24): 50 employees
##     
##     Scope 1 (HQ):
##     - Range: 192.168.1.100 - 192.168.1.250
##     - Hosts: _______ (151 available)
##     - Lease: 8 hours
##     
##     Scope 2 (Branch A):
##     - Range: 192.168.2.100 - 192.168.2.200
##     - Hosts: _______ (101 available)
##     - Lease: 8 hours
##     
##     Scope 3 (Branch B):
##     - Range: 192.168.3.100 - 192.168.3.150
##     - Hosts: _______ (51 available)
##     - Lease: 8 hours

## Q8: DHCP options:
##     Common options:
##     - Option 3: _______ (default gateway, e.g., 192.168.1.1)
##     - Option 6: _______ (DNS servers, e.g., 8.8.8.8, 8.8.4.4)
##     - Option 15: _______ (domain name, e.g., techcorp.com)
##     - Option 42: _______ (NTP server for time sync)
##     - Option 44: _______ (WINS servers for NetBIOS)
##     
##     Configuration:
##     - Set in scope options
##     - Applied to all clients in scope
##     - Can be overridden per client

## PART 3: DHCP RESERVATIONS & EXCLUSIONS
## Q9: DHCP reservations:
##     What: _______ (permanent IP allocation by MAC address)
##     
##     Use cases:
##     - Print server: Always 192.168.1.10
##     - Wireless AP: Always 192.168.1.20
##     - File server: Always 192.168.1.30
##     - Reason: _______ (external systems need fixed IPs)
##     
##     Configuration:
##     - MAC address: 00:11:22:33:44:55 (printer)
##     - Reserved IP: 192.168.1.10
##     - Always assigned: Even after reboot
##     - Benefit: _______ (centralized IP management)

## Q10: DHCP exclusions:
##      What: _______ (addresses NOT assigned by DHCP)
##      
##      Types:
##      - Range exclusion: 192.168.1.1 - 192.168.1.50
##      - Single exclusion: 192.168.1.5 (DNS)
##      
##      Example:
##      - Scope: 192.168.1.0/24 (256 addresses)
##      - Exclude: 192.168.1.1 - 192.168.1.99 (99 addresses)
##      - Available: 192.168.1.100 - 192.168.1.254 (155 addresses)
##      - Reason: _______ (reserve for static IPs)

## PART 4: DHCP RELAY & REMOTE SUBNETS
## Q11: DHCP relay agent:
##      Problem: _______ (DHCP broadcast doesn't cross routers)
##      - Broadcast domain: Limited to local subnet
##      - Remote subnets: Cannot reach DHCP server
##      - Solution: DHCP relay agent
##      
##      Relay process:
##      1. Client broadcasts DHCPDISCOVER
##      2. Relay intercepts (local router or server)
##      3. Relay converts to unicast
##      4. Relay sends to DHCP server (remote)
##      5. Server responds with offer
##      6. Relay converts back to broadcast
##      7. Client receives offer on local subnet

## Q12: DHCP relay configuration:
##      Scenario: HQ has DHCP server, branches need DHCP
##      
##      Branch A setup:
##      - Router at 192.168.2.1 (gateway)
##      - Enable DHCP relay on router
##      - Point to: HQ DHCP server (10.0.0.5)
##      - Result: _______ (clients get IPs from HQ)
##      
##      Benefit:
##      - Single server: Centralized management
##      - Lower cost: One DHCP license
##      - Consistency: All sites use same settings

## PART 5: DHCP TROUBLESHOOTING
## Q13: Common DHCP issues:
##      Issue: Client cannot get IP address
##      - Cause 1: _______ (DHCP scope exhausted)
##      - Cause 2: _______ (DHCP server down)
##      - Cause 3: _______ (relay not configured)
##      - Cause 4: _______ (network connectivity issue)
##      
##      Troubleshooting:
##      - Check 1: Is DHCP server running?
##      - Check 2: Are there available IPs in scope?
##      - Check 3: Can client reach DHCP server?
##      - Check 4: Is DHCP relay configured on routers?

## Q14: DHCP scope exhaustion:
##      Problem: All IPs assigned, new clients get error
##      
##      Scenario:
##      - Scope: 192.168.1.100 - 192.168.1.200 (101 addresses)
##      - Leased: 101/101 (100%)
##      - New client: Cannot get IP
##      - Error: "No addresses available"
##      
##      Solutions:
##      - Reduce lease time: _______ (smaller scope timeout)
##      - Add more scope: _______ (extend range)
##      - Remove reservations: _______ (free up IPs)
##      - Increase servers: _______ (secondary server)

## Q15: DHCP client issues:
##      Issue: Device has APIPA address (169.254.x.x)
##      - Meaning: _______ (no DHCP server reachable)
##      - Cause: Network connectivity, DHCP down
##      - Solution: Renew lease (ipconfig /renew)
##      
##      Issue: Device using wrong subnet
##      - Symptom: _______ (address from different scope)
##      - Cause: Relay misconfiguration
##      - Solution: Check relay settings

## Q16: Monitoring DHCP:
##      Metrics:
##      - Scope utilization: _______ (% IPs in use)
##      - Lease count: How many active leases?
##      - Fail rate: Failed lease requests?
##      - Renewal rate: How often renewed?
##      
##      Alerts:
##      - Utilization >80%: _______ (scope nearly full)
##      - Server down: _______ (no DHCP available)
##      - Unusual activity: DoS or attack?

## PART 6: DHCP BEST PRACTICES
## Q17: DHCP server placement:
##      Single-site deployment:
##      - One server: Sufficient for < 100 devices
##      - Primary/secondary: Recommended (redundancy)
##      - Colocation: _______ (same room as equipment)
##      
##      Multi-site deployment:
##      - Option 1: Central server + relay agents
##      - Option 2: _______ (distributed servers per site)
##      - Recommended: Central (easier management)

## Q18: Scope sizing:
##      Question: "How big should DHCP scope be?"
##      
##      Formula:
##      - Current devices: 100
##      - Growth rate: 20% per year
##      - Planning period: 3 years
##      - Future: 100 × 1.2^3 = _______ (173 devices)
##      - Scope size: _______ (200 addresses, 15% buffer)

## Q19: Security best practices:
##      DHCP snooping:
##      - Purpose: _______ (prevent rogue DHCP servers)
##      - Configuration: Whitelist trusted ports
##      - Untrusted ports: Reject DHCP responses
##      - Benefit: _______ (prevent IP hijacking)
##      
##      IP source guard:
##      - Purpose: _______ (ensure IP matches reserved MAC)
##      - Configuration: Combine with DHCP snooping
##      - Effect: Block IP spoofing attacks

## Q20: DHCP documentation:
##      Must document:
##      - Scope ranges: _______ (all subnets, ranges)
##      - Reservations: _______ (MAC/IP pairs)
##      - Relay configuration: _______ (which routers)
##      - Options: _______ (DNS, gateway, NTP)
##      - Failover: _______ (primary/secondary setup)
##      - Contacts: _______ (administrator names, phones)
solution:
# DHCP SOLUTION

## PART 1: DHCP FUNDAMENTALS

### What is DHCP?

**Definition**: **DYNAMIC HOST CONFIGURATION PROTOCOL** ✓
**Purpose**: **AUTOMATICALLY ASSIGN IP ADDRESSES** ✓
**Benefits**: **NO MANUAL CONFIGURATION, CENTRALIZED** ✓

**Components**:
- Server: Maintains pool of addresses
- Client: Requests IP on startup
- Process: Allocation, lease, renewal

### DHCP vs Static IP

**Static IP** (manual):
- Assignment: **ADMINISTRATOR CONFIGURES**
- Flexibility: Low (change = reconfigure)
- Reliability: High (won't change)
- Use: Servers, printers (need consistent address)
- Admin burden: **HIGH, EVERY DEVICE**

**Dynamic IP** (DHCP):
- Assignment: **SERVER AUTOMATICALLY ASSIGNS** ✓
- Flexibility: High (roaming, temporary devices)
- Reliability: Depends on DHCP server
- Use: Desktops, laptops, temporary devices
- Admin burden: **LOW, CENTRALIZED** ✓

### DHCP Process (DORA)

**Step 1 - Discover**: **CLIENT BROADCASTS REQUEST** ✓
- Packet: "Any DHCP servers out there?"
- Scope: Broadcast to entire subnet
- Message type: DHCPDISCOVER

**Step 2 - Offer**: **SERVER OFFERS ADDRESS** ✓
- Packet: "You can use 192.168.1.100 for 8 hours"
- Sent: Unicast to client
- Message type: DHCPOFFER

**Step 3 - Request**: **CLIENT ACCEPTS OFFER** ✓
- Packet: "I accept 192.168.1.100"
- Scope: Broadcast (other servers cancel offers)
- Message type: DHCPREQUEST

**Step 4 - Acknowledge**: **SERVER CONFIRMS** ✓
- Packet: "Confirmed, lease until 10 AM"
- Sent: Unicast to client
- Message type: DHCPACK

### DHCP Lease Process

**Lease duration**: **TYPICALLY 8 HOURS TO 7 DAYS** ✓

**Timeline**:
- T=0: IP assigned, lease begins
- T=50% (4 hours): Renewal attempt (T1)
- T=87.5% (7 hours): Rebinding attempt (T2)
- T=100% (8 hours): Lease expires

**Renewal process**:
- Attempt: Contact original DHCP server
- If available: Renew lease (extends 8 more hours) ✓
- If unavailable: Try other servers (rebinding)
- If no response: Keep using IP (grace period)
- Expiration: Release IP after lease ends

### DHCP Scope Configuration

**Network**: 192.168.1.0/24 (256 total addresses)

**Scope configuration**:
- Start address: **192.168.1.100** ✓
- End address: **192.168.1.200** ✓
- Range: 101 addresses available
- Reserved: Gateway (192.168.1.1), etc.
- Lease duration: **8 HOURS** ✓

**Exclusion list**:
- 192.168.1.1: Gateway
- 192.168.1.5: DNS server
- 192.168.1.10: Print server
- Reason: These need fixed IPs

## PART 2: DHCP SERVER CONFIGURATION

### DHCP Server Roles

**Primary server**:
- Role: **MAIN SERVER, OWNS SCOPES** ✓
- Availability: Must be up
- Failure: Network cannot get IPs
- Backup: Secondary server needed

**Secondary server**:
- Role: **BACKUP, SHARES SCOPES** ✓
- Availability: Activated if primary fails
- Configuration: Mirrors primary scopes
- Split: Typically 70/30 or 80/20

**Failover**:
- 80/20 split: Primary has 80%, secondary 20%
- Reason: **FASTER PRIMARY RESPONSE** ✓
- Redundancy: If primary fails, 20% still available

### DHCP Scope for Multiple Subnets

**TechCorp offices**:
- HQ (192.168.1.0/24): 200 employees
- Branch A (192.168.2.0/24): 100 employees
- Branch B (192.168.3.0/24): 50 employees

**Scope 1** (HQ):
- Range: 192.168.1.100 - 192.168.1.250
- Hosts: **151 AVAILABLE** ✓
- Lease: 8 hours

**Scope 2** (Branch A):
- Range: 192.168.2.100 - 192.168.2.200
- Hosts: **101 AVAILABLE** ✓
- Lease: 8 hours

**Scope 3** (Branch B):
- Range: 192.168.3.100 - 192.168.3.150
- Hosts: **51 AVAILABLE** ✓
- Lease: 8 hours

### DHCP Options

**Common options**:
- Option 3: **DEFAULT GATEWAY** (e.g., 192.168.1.1) ✓
- Option 6: **DNS SERVERS** (e.g., 8.8.8.8, 8.8.4.4) ✓
- Option 15: **DOMAIN NAME** (e.g., techcorp.com) ✓
- Option 42: **NTP SERVER FOR TIME SYNC** ✓
- Option 44: **WINS SERVERS FOR NetBIOS** ✓

**Configuration**:
- Set in scope options
- Applied to all clients in scope
- Can be overridden per client

## PART 3: DHCP RESERVATIONS & EXCLUSIONS

### DHCP Reservations

**What**: **PERMANENT IP ALLOCATION BY MAC ADDRESS** ✓

**Use cases**:
- Print server: Always 192.168.1.10
- Wireless AP: Always 192.168.1.20
- File server: Always 192.168.1.30
- Reason: **EXTERNAL SYSTEMS NEED FIXED IPS** ✓

**Configuration**:
- MAC address: 00:11:22:33:44:55 (printer)
- Reserved IP: 192.168.1.10
- Always assigned: Even after reboot ✓
- Benefit: **CENTRALIZED IP MANAGEMENT** ✓

### DHCP Exclusions

**What**: **ADDRESSES NOT ASSIGNED BY DHCP** ✓

**Types**:
- Range exclusion: 192.168.1.1 - 192.168.1.50
- Single exclusion: 192.168.1.5 (DNS)

**Example**:
- Scope: 192.168.1.0/24 (256 addresses)
- Exclude: 192.168.1.1 - 192.168.1.99 (99 addresses)
- Available: 192.168.1.100 - 192.168.1.254 (155 addresses)
- Reason: **RESERVE FOR STATIC IPS** ✓

## PART 4: DHCP RELAY & REMOTE SUBNETS

### DHCP Relay Agent

**Problem**: **DHCP BROADCAST DOESN'T CROSS ROUTERS** ⚠️
- Broadcast domain: Limited to local subnet
- Remote subnets: Cannot reach DHCP server
- Solution: DHCP relay agent

**Relay process**:
1. Client broadcasts DHCPDISCOVER
2. Relay intercepts (local router or server)
3. Relay converts to unicast ✓
4. Relay sends to DHCP server (remote)
5. Server responds with offer
6. Relay converts back to broadcast
7. Client receives offer on local subnet

### DHCP Relay Configuration

**Scenario**: HQ has DHCP server, branches need DHCP

**Branch A setup**:
- Router at 192.168.2.1 (gateway)
- Enable DHCP relay on router
- Point to: HQ DHCP server (10.0.0.5)
- Result: **CLIENTS GET IPS FROM HQ** ✓

**Benefit**:
- Single server: Centralized management
- Lower cost: One DHCP license
- Consistency: All sites use same settings

## PART 5: DHCP TROUBLESHOOTING

### Common DHCP Issues

**Issue**: Client cannot get IP address
- Cause 1: **DHCP SCOPE EXHAUSTED** ⚠️
- Cause 2: **DHCP SERVER DOWN** ⚠️
- Cause 3: **RELAY NOT CONFIGURED** ⚠️
- Cause 4: **NETWORK CONNECTIVITY ISSUE** ⚠️

**Troubleshooting**:
- Check 1: Is DHCP server running?
- Check 2: Are there available IPs in scope?
- Check 3: Can client reach DHCP server?
- Check 4: Is DHCP relay configured on routers?

### DHCP Scope Exhaustion

**Problem**: All IPs assigned, new clients get error

**Scenario**:
- Scope: 192.168.1.100 - 192.168.1.200 (101 addresses)
- Leased: 101/101 (100%)
- New client: Cannot get IP
- Error: "No addresses available"

**Solutions**:
- Reduce lease time: **SMALLER SCOPE TIMEOUT** ✓
- Add more scope: **EXTEND RANGE** ✓
- Remove reservations: **FREE UP IPS** ✓
- Increase servers: **SECONDARY SERVER** ✓

### DHCP Client Issues

**Issue**: Device has APIPA address (169.254.x.x)
- Meaning: **NO DHCP SERVER REACHABLE** ⚠️
- Cause: Network connectivity, DHCP down
- Solution: Renew lease (ipconfig /renew)

**Issue**: Device using wrong subnet
- Symptom: **ADDRESS FROM DIFFERENT SCOPE** ⚠️
- Cause: Relay misconfiguration
- Solution: Check relay settings

### Monitoring DHCP

**Metrics**:
- Scope utilization: **% IPS IN USE** ✓
- Lease count: How many active leases?
- Fail rate: Failed lease requests?
- Renewal rate: How often renewed?

**Alerts**:
- Utilization >80%: **SCOPE NEARLY FULL** ⚠️
- Server down: **NO DHCP AVAILABLE** ⚠️
- Unusual activity: DoS or attack?

## PART 6: DHCP BEST PRACTICES

### DHCP Server Placement

**Single-site deployment**:
- One server: Sufficient for < 100 devices
- Primary/secondary: Recommended (redundancy) ✓
- Colocation: **SAME ROOM AS EQUIPMENT** ✓

**Multi-site deployment**:
- Option 1: Central server + relay agents
- Option 2: **DISTRIBUTED SERVERS PER SITE** ✓
- Recommended: Central (easier management)

### Scope Sizing

**Question**: "How big should DHCP scope be?"

**Formula**:
- Current devices: 100
- Growth rate: 20% per year
- Planning period: 3 years
- Future: 100 × 1.2^3 = **173 DEVICES** ✓
- Scope size: **200 ADDRESSES, 15% BUFFER** ✓

### Security Best Practices

**DHCP snooping**:
- Purpose: **PREVENT ROGUE DHCP SERVERS** ✓
- Configuration: Whitelist trusted ports
- Untrusted ports: Reject DHCP responses
- Benefit: **PREVENT IP HIJACKING** ✓

**IP source guard**:
- Purpose: **ENSURE IP MATCHES RESERVED MAC** ✓
- Configuration: Combine with DHCP snooping
- Effect: Block IP spoofing attacks

### DHCP Documentation

**Must document**:
- Scope ranges: **ALL SUBNETS, RANGES** ✓
- Reservations: **MAC/IP PAIRS** ✓
- Relay configuration: **WHICH ROUTERS** ✓
- Options: **DNS, GATEWAY, NTP** ✓
- Failover: **PRIMARY/SECONDARY SETUP** ✓
- Contacts: **ADMINISTRATOR NAMES, PHONES** ✓
hints: DHCP = Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol; automates IP assignment via DORA (Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledge)
DHCP process: Client broadcasts → Server offers → Client requests → Server acknowledges
Lease renewal at 50% (T1) and 87.5% (T2); if primary unavailable, try secondary (rebinding)
Scope = range of IPs available; excludes network, broadcast, gateway, and reserved addresses
DHCP options: Option 3 (gateway), Option 6 (DNS), Option 15 (domain), Option 42 (NTP)
Reservations = permanent IP allocation by MAC address (for servers, printers, APs)
DHCP relay agent converts broadcast to unicast to reach DHCP server on remote subnet
Scope exhaustion (no IPs available): Reduce lease, extend scope, remove reservations, add secondary server
APIPA address (169.254.x.x) = no DHCP server reachable; troubleshoot connectivity or server
DHCP snooping prevents rogue servers; IP source guard prevents IP spoofing attacks

testCases.description: Should correctly explain dhcp definition concepts and implementation details
testCases.validation: assert ('dhcp' in solution.lower() or 'dynamic' in solution.lower() or 'ip' in solution.lower())

testCases.description: Should correctly explain dhcp process concepts and implementation details

testCases.description: Should correctly explain lease management concepts and implementation details
